item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: scp 2197-5 actually made me laugh, no feedback here but this is my new favorite sub-anomaly

item.title: Really solid
item.content:encoded:
I smiled when I got the thing about the letters, then I felt got a sinking feeling when I read the last two entries. What a subtle way to do a huge twist.

As others have noted, the containment procedures are well thought out and original. This sounds like how a real covert agency would handle things.

Also, I tremendously appreciate such a fresh take on a classic trope. Creepy shops are timeless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a really fun read. One nit pick I have is there isnt enough pictures. It would be cool to have pictures showing the items produced by 2197.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely adore wholesome skips like this, and I love your explanation on it. I'd love to perhaps see some tales about the students or people who purchase the items. This is like something out of a 2000s urban fantasy book, and it makes me smile. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although what you're talking about was written before the article was published, it wasn't adapted into an anime episode until 2016, so probably not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is SCP-2197-13 A monogatari reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was all fun and games until I read -27… Why couldn't it stay all fun and games?
All jokes aside, great article, seems malevolent until you read the actual objects (well, except the last one)
Also, where can I get that -25 book? I need it for reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor -25's item author. Probably tried so hard to make an anomaly affecting luck and probability. But no, his teacher just had to rate this "WAW", probably because it's not fitting into his subjective idea of what demonic art should look like. Also, I can't believe that last item was made by the student himself. Looks like his parents helped him in some way.

I can see demons suffering from the same education problems as we do. In any way, it was fun to read. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not-So-Needful Things!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good one that made me smile a little bit.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was just reading through this entry and until the descriptions of the items it did not manage to catch my interest and upvote.

The annoying items at first were amusing, so I did not expect the last one to be this excellent.

+1

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
Hope you enjoy it!

https://youtu.be/hxIorQFomx4

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Solomoni actually already are used elsewhere in a different context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I doubt anything else will happen with this article at this point, but I just had the thought that the glyphs could be located on stickers with the word Scholomance printed on them in a small font, in order to give another clue to the educational origin of the items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't be the first time. (Warning: TVTropes link)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article, I liked it more when I realized what the glyphs were for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, plus one.

Second, I want that coloring book. Or just a page. One page to color. I don't even care if it has a unicorn on it, or a rainbow. Or both. Hell, if was a coloring book filled with nothing but pro-brony propaganda, I would still want a page.

Gib it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really strike me as much more than another Log of Anomalous Items that happens to be curated in a village in Maine. I realize that for a lot of people, the setting was something that added to the story, and obviously this is a successful article, so I wouldn't recommend changing it. But to me, it came across as extraneous and stapled-on. Taken on its own, it's just another "this place be cursed" local legend type thing, complete with spooky compulsions to strand people near it. The level of detail is enough to make me wonder what the connection is to the later developments of the article, but not enough to tie it to those developments in any discernible way. As a result, it comes off as kind of extraneous means of getting to the point of the article, which is "long list of crazy stuff".

It's not terrible, and the clock thing was so crazily over the top that I couldn't help but chuckle when I read it, but it doesn't really feel like a fully-realized article to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's with everyone hating on D-Class termination all of a sudden?

This has been steadily changing over at least the last year. This isn't a sudden thing.

You don't have to use 'em, but why on earth would you go out of your way to object to referencing a thing that ~99% of the site generally includes in their headcanon

No. "99%" of the site does not, in fact, include monthly D-Class termination into their vision of the Foundation. There has been a significant portion of the site that has objected to this for quite a while.

That's mostly because, I suspect, the act of killing people for no good reason makes absolutely no goddamn sense. It's a testament to the quality of the site that we're capable of reevaluating commonly appearing tropes and literary devices with an eye towards improving quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's with everyone hating on D-Class termination all of a sudden? It's such an established piece of the universe that complaining about it is on par with complaining about the existence of SCP-682, the Overseers, or Clef…

You don't have to use 'em, but why on earth would you go out of your way to object to referencing a thing that ~99% of the site generally includes in their headcanon, if not directly into their stories or articles?

Also, if we're going to worry about being gay-inclusive for a 2-person sample set, shouldn't we include a whole collapsible dedicated to letting a bunch of D-Class escape, since knowing how it impacts all possible flavors of sexuality is obviously more important than little things like operational security?

…Okay, that was pretty sarcastic, but from a scientific standpoint they only have data on two couples escaping. They don't know how it will affect different sexual preferences, so why would they worry about being politically correct? That just removes a data point. The two couples were straight. They wrote that down and didn't test it further because, y'know, the D-Class escaped. That's probably not something they want to encourage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what? D-Class termination is an enormous part of Foundation lore. You can work around it in your own pieces if you like, but please don't bash on authors for referencing things that are very, very well-established just because you don't personally care for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see how it would make it any more obvious than the inclusion of the concept as a whole. It just saves the reader the trouble of googling it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems to come from the same silly hellscape that 2980 comes from. While I didn't care for that article in the least, I actually find myself less annoyed with this one. I think it's because the item log does a great job at portraying what exactly the class and its participants are like, although maybe one or two of the log entries feels a bit superfluous.

The manner of containment is definitely smart. A lesser writer might just go with the ol' "agents surround the perimeter, amnestics for any civilian who stumbles upon it" approach and call it a day, but you clearly actually thought about your containment procedures.

I have problems with the grading system presented here, similar to Dmatix's. Seems more like it was made this way because it was the easiest way to hint towards your intentions with the story, rather than because it was the most fitting/logical grading system that such a class would use.

Going with a no-vote because I'm just not a fan of otherworldly/alien cultures being so damn human, sometimes even if that's the point. I can concede to that sort of stuff if it's sufficiently entertaining, but this one just misses the mark for me. Good job, though.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
You've veered off from the article and started to try and speak for the entirety of the human race.

Stop. You're done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because most opposite-sex "attachments" (in how the word is normally used) are romantic. Most same-sex "attachments" are simply a close relationship.

Just the way human people work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope that doesn't make it too easy. I wouldn't want to sacrifice the Foundation's longstanding traditions of non-transparency just because I was too lazy to look it up…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I said I'd stop replying but I gotta call bullshit on one point:

It doesn't, though. It simply deletes the opposite-sex reference. The word "Attachment" in this context is very vague, otherwise.

It's understood as-is that it's romantic attachment. Deleting the opposite-sex reference doesn't change that. Why are you saying it does?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y-you realize you can have articles that include non-white non-hetereo people without making the article dedicated to non-white non-hetero people, right? O_o

Sure. My point wasn't "Inclusion of non-WASPs (or Catholics) makes it about them." My point was "Knocking it because a reference to X minority (racial, sexual, religious, whatever) could've been included and was instead absent isn't a legit complaint".

If the author of *had* included them I wouldn't've complained. (and would be having a parallel discussion with anyone who did) I'm not saying they *shouldn't* be there. I'm saying there's no reason that they *should*. It's simply a matter of writing choice to include any type of people at this point, and he chose not too.

Decibelle literally posted an example edit that would've conveyed the stereotype of love equally quickly if not faster.

It doesn't, though. It simply deletes the opposite-sex reference. The word "Attachment" in this context is very vague, otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liking the concept. My only suggestion is that you add the names of the letters in order somewhere, like "backward renderings of the first, second, third, fourth, and sixth letters of the Aramaic alphabet (alap, _, _, etc)." I feel like that might make it more obvious it's a grading system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this a fair bit, but is anyone else getting a "Needful Things" vibe from this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most importantly, the article doesn't include specific mentions of ethnic minorities, foreigners, or non-Catholic religions. That's not what the article is for.

Y-you realize you can have articles that include non-white non-hetereo people without making the article dedicated to non-white non-hetero people, right? O_o

The author is trying to quickly convey an idea, stereotypes are the fastest way to do that.

Decibelle literally posted an example edit that would've conveyed the stereotype of love equally quickly if not faster.

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a weird little curio shop full of anomalous items is great. It is a trope but a good one because when you are in a real antique shop you get the feeling there is probably something like that in there.
Let me suggest an addition; a test log or some other document listing purchases from the shop that others can edit adding new items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is correct. And in fact, in that reply you quoted and in the following reply to the author, I was pointing out how this made the story read awkwardly and showed how, if the author so chose to, they could make it read easier and better.

I'll admit; I initially started that critique on that specific point because really, it's tiring seeing heterosexual narratives shoved into places where they have no reason to be. However, if that was all my critique of that point was for, it'd be worthless. That's why I expanded and elaborated on that bit. I think though, it's up to the author, and if they don't wanna change it, it's their call. As it stands, this skip is really damn good.

I think continuing this won't get us much elsewhere, though I am always free in PM and chat to talk about stuff like this. And I guess as future reference, I know you didn't want it to seem personal, but I wouldn't mind being confronted on it to spurn a discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the point is less "spread the homogay agenda muahaha" and more "there's no real reason to specify that the people running away were always straight". And that point she made was only a small part of the overall critique. Also, there's several articles o the site that mix genders of D-class liberally, so that's a non-factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y NO LIKE BUTTON!

That edit was hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how to say this to you, but "hey, the article doesn't account for the fact that straight people are not the only people" isn't really unnecessarily politically correct.

Why *should* the article include more than a stereotypical fairy-tale sequence? The author is trying to quickly convey an idea, stereotypes are the fastest way to do that. And given the Abrahamic theme, why would a demon taking the assignment in completely the wrong way include homosexual couples?

Most importantly, the article doesn't include specific mentions of ethnic minorities, foreigners, or non-Catholic religions. That's not what the article is for.

Yes, a small part of it was a personal point of contention, but I didn't let that inform my criticism of that bit

Except it was the core of your critique on this point:

along with a D-class of the opposite sex with whom the subject had recently formed an attachment.

So there can't be gay relationships formed? So gay people are immune to this? Why does it have to be opposite sex? This is a slightly bigger point of contention for me personally.

I think the largest issue for me is that the report is a report from 2 tests on "what happened." Not an analysis on the mechanics or details of how it works. And "The D-Class death-row criminals are escaping" is more than enough reason to suspend future testing. 2 tests would, based solely on probability and the subject pool, be almost guaranteed hetero. What's more, if Foundation D-Class incarceration protocols are at all similar to actual prisons, the gender segregation was extensive, giving the item an extra 'oomph' to those who know how it works.

So there's no real justification for the comment itself other than personal agenda or activism.

As for the oblique reference, I wanted to make the statement without making it personal. Sorry for the confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though I realize this is pedantry at its finest, I do feel the need to comment that the inclusion of plus and minus signs next to letters from the Aramaic alphabet (backwards or otherwise) seems fairly nonsensical. Needless to say, Aramaic never used plus and minus signs, which only began to appear in writing much, much later, and in completely different areas of the world. There is record of similar signs existing in the same general area and time period, but those are not our plus and minus signs.

Also, you make it seem like realizing the runes are backwards Aramaic is some grand feat by the Foundation's linguists, when it really shouldn't be. The alphabet is certainly well known enough that any serious ancient linguist wouldn't blink once before realizing that.

item.title: Re: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded: Honestly, if a reader who doesn't even know any Aramaic (e.g. me) can figure it out, I'd actually be pretty surprised if the Foundation's researchers didn't hit on the explanation. At that point, just ask the shopkeeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem with this is that yes, hemoglobin is largely the reason blood is red, but it's also a completely different substance than blood and has distinctly different properties. Chances are good that it's nowhere near as lasting or as effective a stain as whole blood.

Unfortunately, there don't seem to be a lot of accurate but non-obvious synonyms for "blood". You're kinda stuck with "written in a blood-like substance" unless you're willing to resort to something like "written in an organic red-brown pigment".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But my original post was not a response to you. It was a objection to the unnecessary insertion of politically-correct verbiage to a piece of writing, where the suggested change runs counter to the concept of the story, but promotes or indulges someone else's activism.

I don't know how to say this to you, but "hey, the article doesn't account for the fact that straight people are not the only people" isn't really unnecessarily politically correct. Unless you mean reality is unnecessarily politically correct. Then, sure, I guess. Fair enough.

the suggested change runs counter to the concept of the story

Wait… how does non-straight people run counter to the concept of demon academy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't being "politically correct" or an "activist", as my point was legitmate on 1) wording reasons 2) structural reasons 3) narrative reasons. Yes, a small part of it was a personal point of contention, but I didn't let that inform my crticism of that bit, and I'd really like if you reply to me directly in the future instead of responding to me in other comments. I'm not trying to have the author "indulge" in my activism or whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also:

Site-11’s Shamanic Analysis Unit has concluded that the effect targets only unsuspecting individuals.

might be better as

analysis from Site-11 has concluded that the effect targets only unsuspecting individuals.

item.title: Re: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded:
Would it be more or less confusing if the log entries for the individual items said "Grade: Gamal-minus" instead of "Glyph: Gamal-minus"?

Yeah. Problem is how to make it so the Foundation knows. Maybe a D-Class asks the shopkeeper?

"Hey man, what's the squiggly thing right here?"

"That's the scoring that the maker received from his instructor."

"Oh. Is it good or bad?"

"Quite bad, perhaps I could interest you in something that would better suit your tastes?"

"Nah, I think I'm just gonna take the coloring book. Least creepy thing in here."

"If you are certain."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to note - I would dump "second-hand" and replace it with "badly handmade". I agree with everyone else who commented on it that it steers the reader away from what you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I find the writing gimmick of "Here's a word everyone is familiar with and has a particular meaning. The hook is that I define it to mean the opposite of what everyone else does." to be a more convoluted and lazier version of an unreliable narrator. I mean, if you boil it down, it becomes "The hook is I lied."

If the story has a character with horns, hooves, red skin, pointy tail, and smells of sulfur but is actually a nice creature that superficially looks like a demon, fine. (Although that specific example bumps right up on the line, it's intended as hyperbole.) If the story *has* a demon that, until the very end, confirms his demon status and then oops he's really not-demon, that's lazy writing.

But we're not talking about anything of this nature in this SCP. Up until the clock, it could've simply been evil gnomes or goblins, but the clock pretty clearly invokes "hellish" as a concept.

But my original post was not a response to you. It was a objection to the unnecessary insertion of politically-correct verbiage to a piece of writing, where the suggested change runs counter to the concept of the story, but promotes or indulges someone else's activism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[I]t could've questioned the straight definition of a demon. Why is it an abrahamic demon academy? There are other "demon" like entities that aren't necessarily "evil" according to the stereotype perception of it. You could have a lot of fun representing a lot of ideas on what a demon is and what evil is and what a demonic pet project is, but here it's very laid out and exactly as expected given the thing.

That would be a fine thing to do. It just wasn't the goal in this case. The goal was to veer into parody and then veer back at the end with a reminder that, oh yeah, even though this is being played for laughs, the original trope (a shop that sells cursed items to the unsuspecting) is still a pretty horrific concept, which is why it became a trope in the first place.

item.title: Re: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded: As an Argentinean, I can confirm that ,if you replace the Z with a C, anciano means elder in spanish.

item.title: Re: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded: As an Italian, I can say that anziano does mean elder.

item.title: Re: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded:
I originally named him Mr. Elder, but that seemed too spot on. I changed it to "anziano" which Google Translate says is Italian for "elder." It's not ideal. I'm open to other suggestions for the name.

Would it help if he referred to the items as "projects"? Would that be a clue that's not too much of a clue?

Dammit, you're right about the bookends. Let me think about how to change it.

The effects of the coloring book need to be over the top to really drive home the point that this student completely missed the concept of "cursed object" by the widest margin possible.

EDIT: Would it be more or less confusing if the log entries for the individual items said "Grade: Gamal-minus" instead of "Glyph: Gamal-minus"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the point of this SCP, the Foundation, and writing in general

Re-interpreting and incorporating SCPs is the whole point of writing. SCP itself is based of re-interpretations of the format of SCP-173. Writing in general is all about interpretating and re-interpreting whatever input happens. The first example that comes to mind is Laird Hunt, an author who is a finalist for the 2013 PEN/Faulkner Award for Fiction for his novel, Kind One.I've spoken with Laird Hunt and he's told me that Kind One was literally a re-interpretation of a throw-away conversation in another novel he read.

Complaining that the results of a demonic shop class don't match up with your personal beliefs

I'm saying that for the initial idea, the execution is lackluster and frankly boring/uncreative. It's based on very stereotypical understandings of good and evil and really doesn't take the initial idea to any new heights. I'm here to be impressed and invested in something, even if it's an idea I've seen before. And given the initial idea and the execution, it's unimpressive and I feel it could've been executed in a far more clever fashion.

For example, it could've questioned the straight definition of a demon. Why is it an abrahamic demon academy? There are other "demon" like entities that aren't necessarily "evil" according to the stereotype perception of it. You could have a lot of fun representing a lot of ideas on what a demon is and what evil is and what a demonic pet project is, but here it's very laid out and exactly as expected given the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one had, it's a skip what makes skips and "Shamanic Analysis Unit" is a little ehh.

On the other, it's got great presence, you amnesticized the archbishop of Boston, the lettering system is clever as heck (if not obvious from the article, but it's the kind of thing I don't mind being told afterward) and I really like the first artifact. It's got a "failed horror movie trope" feel that kind of suffuses the whole thing, and that's what really gets me going about this article. Also, I like the unicorn coloring romance book. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe you could replace staticky with "a voice resembling that of a young girl, with heavy interference,"?

Also, whilst I'm here, seconding Decibelle; the monthly termination isn't something really done anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm honestly not sure if #3 was addressed to my comment up there in particular, or if there's something else I'm missing.

item.title: It definitely needs the backstory
item.content:encoded:
Otherwise it just looks like your standard curio-shop-of-destiny and a list of unfinished SCPs that couldn't be bothered to flesh out. Maybe I wasn't paying attention, but I didn't catch the whole "demonic shop class" thing on the first go. I think I was distracted by the cliché Curio Shoppe frame story to appreciate the items and the grading system.

I actually really like how you've tried to come up with a better solution to the whole "drug the populace" thing and brought in some alternative reasoning. I like the idea of there actually being an Ethics Committee that's a bit more reasonable than what most of the Foundation is willing and ready to do.

This is actually a really neat concept, so to help out I've got some constructive criticism, if I may…

My Notes
chew on this a bit...

Might I suggest re-framing the description to better hint that the shop is really more of the door by which the anomalous items enter our world? Perhaps failed attempts to intercept the items or the owner before they make it to the shop…

Consider changing the description from "various second-hand goods" to something indicating them being crudely hand-crafted. "Second-hand" is kind of a red herring; it puts the reader in the frame of mind that these are items collected from various sources rather than a common origin and it does nothing to draw interest into the objects themselves.

Mr. Anziano is not a particularly demonic name. I know you can't spell it out, but to me it was just foreign, without malevolence. Still, it would work well if some attention were drawn away from establishing himself and the Shoppe as the focus of attention.

Footnote 2's should have left the effects of 2197-2 in Document 2197-101. Also consider relabeling the supplemental documentation (what if Mr. Anziano sells more than 100 items?)

2197-5 was an entertaining failure, but the reference was so spot on until the punch line that it looked like unimaginative writing.

2197-22 I feel like the physical description of the bookends could be more succinct.

Within a week of coloring in the page, the subject invariably meets an ideal romantic partner and enters into a long-term relationship.

At first I found the stated effects of 2197-25 a little too mushy, but in this sentence ran it from blandly positive into straight-up glurge. It read like one of those chain-mails that clutter up message boards, and I was too busy rolling my eyes to appreciate the trope-worthy escape incidents which followed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that you tell me, I definitely like it more than my first read-through. Could use some work though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
before scheduled termination

This is a very big problem, and it completely detracts from the entire article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this pleases me greatly

I don't even need the backstory. I just love that it's a weird place that does weird things and that is has all the trappings of a horror story where the community knows something bad is going on but participates in covering it up as best they can.

"Naw you don't want to go to that shop. It's a bad shop," said the old feller, who stared at you with narrow eyes when you went in there anyway.

Good lord I even buy them noticing that a group moved in to help steer people away and not making a big deal about it. This is so good.

"Yeah them gov'ment fellas finally got wind about the bad shop we don't talk about. But don't say anything. This's top secret, yaknow." *wink*

edit: I even love that with the secret backstory, there's no need for the demonic flea market to give a crap about being contained, they just need to get rid of these class projects and the Foundation is happy to scoop them up straight into containment. Everything about this is my favorite flavor of lighthearted Foundation. All the scare is there for you to assemble yourself in your head if you wish, by dreaming up some poor sod who gets an A+ project.

edit2: "I tried to warn them, I looked them straight in the eye and said 'That there is a devil's shop, friend. Everything you can buy in there is cursed and will come after you like something out of a Stephen King book, only one of his crappy newer ones,' but that guy just said 'Cool!' and went right in. Now he has a clock that won't stop screaming."

"It's not all bad, you hear about that one guy who came back with his new wife? Said they got a lucky coloring book that saved them from some men in black types. They came back to buy something else and it cured their smoking!"

"What, really? Maybe I should go give that place a try myself."

And then a very patient demon got an A+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For "staticky", you might go with "a voice partially obscured by static" or something similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should write those up and turn them into either a tale or supplemental documentation. =D
